items.title: Rewrite Python bindings from ctypes to nanobind-based HGS-CVRP integration (v0.1.0)
items.body:
## Summary
This PR rewrites the Python/C++ binding layer from `ctypes` to a compiled `nanobind` extension, while preserving the existing `Solver`/`AlgorithmParameters` interface for backward compatibility.

## What Changed
- Replaced dynamic library loading and `ctypes` structs/calls with a native `nanobind` module: `hybgensea/_core.cpp`.
- Added direct Python APIs:
  - `solve_cvrp(...)`
  - `solve_cvrp_dist_mtx(...)`
  - `solve_tsp(...)`
- Kept legacy compatibility:
  - `AlgorithmParameters` dataclass still available.
  - `Solver.solve_cvrp()` and `Solver.solve_tsp()` now delegate to the new nanobind-backed functions.
- Added log capture/callback support (`log_callback`) and solver log return in `RoutingSolution.log`.
- Released the Python GIL during solver execution to improve concurrency behavior.
- Updated build system:
  - Added `nanobind` to build requirements.
  - CMake now builds and installs `_core` extension.
  - Switched embedded HGS dependency to `mdealencar/HGS-CVRP` commit `0b448216...`.
- Bumped package version from `0.0.1` to `0.1.0`.
- Updated README with fork motivation and migration note to nanobind-based implementation (0.1.0+).

## Why
- Improve performance and reduce Python/C interop overhead.
- Provide a cleaner, typed native extension path.
- Enable better runtime behavior (GIL release, log streaming callback).
- Keep existing user-facing API largely stable during the migration.

## Notes / Compatibility
- Core solver algorithm/default parameters remain aligned with previous behavior.
- Existing code using `Solver` and `AlgorithmParameters` should continue to work.
- Internal implementation is now extension-module based, so local builds/tests require `_core` to be built.

items.title: Implement recommendations from the Port Swigger staff
items.body:
- Any JOptionPane dialogs in your extension are created with a null parent frame, which causes poor window management on multi-monitor setups and incorrect window stacking. Passing the Burp Suite frame as the parent would fix this — you can obtain it via montoyaApi.userInterface().swingUtils().suiteFrame().

- When downloading external configuration files, your sendRequest() call does not pass RequestOptions with upstream TLS verification enabled. This means the TLS certificate chain is not validated, which could allow man-in-the-middle attacks when updating rules. Passing RequestOptions.requestOptions().withVerifyUpstreamTls() would fix this.

- The persistenceExecutor in your results panel is never shut down when the extension unloads. Your other three executors are properly cleaned up in the unload handler so adding the same shutdown call for this executor would prevent the thread leak.

- Your settings UI is registered as a suite tab, but since it only contains configuration fields and options, it should be registered as a settings panel using api.userInterface().registerSettingsPanel() instead. This keeps configuration in the expected location within Burp's UI. Your issues/findings tab is fine as a suite tab.

items.title: Switch from python-ssdeep to simple ctypes wrapper over libfuzzy2
items.body:
<!-- Thank you for contributing! -->
<!-- Please fill this template before submitting your PR (fill the boxes using "x") -->

**Your checklist for this pull request**
- [x] I've read the [contributing guideline](CONTRIBUTING.md).
- [x] I've tested my changes by building and running the project, and testing changed functionality (if applicable)
- [ ] I've added automated tests for my change (if applicable, optional)
- [x] I've updated documentation to reflect my change (if applicable)

**What is the current behaviour?**
<!-- Explain how the code works currently -->

https://pypi.org/project/ssdeep/ is unmaintained and last release was on 2019. We already had issues with setuptools that removed pkg_resources which broke the building process (https://github.com/CERT-Polska/mwdb-core/pull/1137). 

python-ssdeep requires `libfuzzy-dev` because during installation it is building a native Python extension using cffi that is linking with libfuzzy. On the other hand, libfuzzy has quite simple and stable interface which can be easily called using classic ctypes wrapper.

We don't do anything useful with these hashes as we don't support fuzzy search/comparison but this hash is there since initial release, so we maintain it for compatibility reasons.

**What is the new behaviour?**
<!-- Explain how the code works after your changes -->

This PR:
- adds `mwdb.core.ssdeep` with ctypes-based binding to `libfuzzy.so.2` and `SsdeepHash` class
- removes `ssdeep` dependency
- removes build-time dependencies from Docker files, because we no longer need to compile anything (actually most of them were stale)

libfuzzy2 is a dependency of libfuzzy-dev, but it would be good to clearly indicate in the release notes that we switch to another solution and things may break (although they shouldn't).

In case of problems on some platforms, this PR adds `enable_ssdeep` configuration option that allows to disable ssdeep evaluation.

**Test plan**

- Check if ssdeep still produces the same result
- Check if MWDB doesn't break when ssdeep is turned off and produces `null` value in the ssdeep column on file upload

items.title: fix: correct INPUT struct size causing mouse freeze on Windows
items.body:
## Problem

Running the packaged app on Windows causes the mouse to freeze/become unresponsive.

## Root Cause

### Bug 1: `INPUT` struct size mismatch in `_send_ctrl_c()`

The `INPUT` union in `acquisition.py` only contained `KEYBDINPUT`, making `sizeof(INPUT)` **32 instead of the correct 40 bytes** on 64-bit Windows. When `SendInput(4, inputs, sizeof(INPUT))` read the 4-element array with the wrong stride, it interpreted garbage bytes as `MOUSEINPUT` events, injecting random mouse movements/clicks and causing the cursor to freeze.

This bug triggers whenever text acquisition falls back to the clipboard strategy (Strategy 3), which is common in modern applications where UIA and Win32Msg strategies fail.

### Bug 2: Unnecessary work in mouse hook callback

`_handle_mouse_event()` performed `ctypes.cast()` on **every** mouse event (including hundreds of `WM_MOUSEMOVE` per second), but only needed to handle `WM_LBUTTONDOWN` and `WM_LBUTTONUP`. The excess GIL contention could cause Windows to silently remove the hook due to timeout.

## Fix

1. **`acquisition.py`**: Added `MOUSEINPUT` and `HARDWAREINPUT` to the `INPUT` union so the struct size matches the Windows SDK definition.
2. **`hooks.py`**: Added early return for irrelevant mouse messages before the `ctypes.cast()` call.

items.title: [bugfix] Rewrite transfer metrics from C++ to Python side
items.body:
Previously, transfer metrics (ops_total, bytes_total) were recorded in the C++ layer (transfer.cu, transfer_ssd.cpp, gds_manager.cpp) via MetricsManager macros. Although C++ has its own Prometheus exporter (port 8081), the actual data transfer code runs in forked worker subprocesses, which do not initialize the C++ Prometheus metrics server. As a result, these transfer metrics were never collected at all.

Key changes:
- Rewrite transfer metrics collection entirely on the Python side with a post-completion approach, recording metrics in the main process after transfer operations complete
- Add `flexkv_py_transfer_bytes_total` counter to Python collector for tracking data transfer throughput
- Remove `flexkv_cpp_transfer_ops_total` and `flexkv_cpp_transfer_bytes_total` counters from C++ MetricsManager

Changes include:
- Extended `CompletedOp` in `flexkv/common/transfer.py` with `transfer_type`, `num_blocks`, and `num_bytes` fields
- Compute transfer bytes in `TransferEngine` (`transfer_engine.py`) when an op finishes
- Record all three counters (ops_total, blocks_total, bytes_total) via `record_transfer_completed()` in `KVTaskManager._update_tasks()` (`kvtask.py`), ensuring metrics are collected in the main process
- Added `flexkv_py_transfer_bytes_total` counter to `flexkv/metrics/collector.py`
- Removed old `_record_transfer_ops()` from `cache_engine.py`
- Removed C++ transfer metric recording from `transfer.cu`, `transfer_ssd.cpp`, and `gds_manager.cpp`
- Updated Grafana dashboard (`flexkv-demo.json`): added Transfer Throughput panel, replaced C++ Transfer Throughput with Cache Blocks Rate panel
- Updated monitoring documentation at `docs/monitoring/README_en.md` (English) and `docs/monitoring/README_zh.md` (Chinese)

items.title: SHARED-12177 patch: toMonomeric: improve monomer recognition
items.body:
* Linked Case: SHARED-12177

### Description

This is just a cherry pick of the mmshare commit below (excluding SWIG changes and Python unit tests)

### Testing done

Build with unit tests.

### Original description

The key change here is the enumerate_tautomers() function, which is used both when generating the core SMILES lookup table for all monomers, and when generating the query molecules for the complex monomers. It is a rudimentary implementation that only handles some common imine-enamine and imidazole tautomerisms, but it is good enough to bring our success rate at generating SMILES-free HELM strings from ChEMBL from 66.7% to 96.4%, while being much faster than the RDKit tautomer canonicalizer with default settings, which was our previous, unsuccessful attempt. Still, this is a work in progress.

There were a few other changes which may seem random, but all are related with improving monomer recognition.

- neutralizeAtoms() now also standardizes the representation of sulfoxides as neutral S=O.
- changed the definition of "complex monomer" to include monomers that match the generic residue pattern only once but have the two attachment points unusually apart (!= 4 bonds)
    - also, allow the pattern to match alpha-disubstituted amino acids
- removed the urea exception from the generic amino acide SMARTS, because those are now handled by the more general complex monomer mechanism
- added a fallback to findHelmSymbol(): if there is no match, try again without removing the terminal atom and/or the R1 attachment point. The reason is that ChEMBL has many custom monomers that lack one or the other even though the SMARTS pattern would match them (typically, N- or O-substituted amino acids that are meant to be terminal)

Incidental changes:
- added a couple of missing SWIG wrappers
- moved a couple of "private" functions that were needed from one than one translation unit to the new monomer_utils.cpp

items.title: fix(python): use native ARM64 precompiled Python on Windows ARM
items.body:
Hello there! 👋 

First and foremost, thank you for creating and actively developing `mise`! 😃 I discovered it through [this HackerNews post](https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=45491621) and I've used it for a personal project since then. By the way, I'm genuinely impressed by how many releases you ship every now and then 👍

Today, I wanted to run my python project on Windows ARM64, and this happened:

```
 DEBUG using lockfile URL for platform windows-arm64: https://github.com/astral-sh/python-build-standalone/releases/download/20260324/cpython-3.13.12+20260324-x86_64-pc-windows-msvc-install_only_stripped.tar.gz
  INFO  python@3.13.12       [2/4] download cpython-3.13.12+20260324-x86_64-pc-windows-msvc-install_only_stripped.tar.gz
```

I saw the resulting failure when trying to load an ARM64 DLL from the downloaded x86_64 Python:

```
  >>> import ctypes; ctypes.CDLL(r"C:\PATH\TO\ARM64\DLL\engine.dll")
  OSError: [WinError 193] %1 is not a valid Win32 application
```

This fix change the downloaded python into: 

```
  DEBUG GET https://mise-versions.jdx.dev/tools/python-precompiled-aarch64-pc-windows-msvc.gz
  INFO  python@3.13.12       [2/4] download cpython-3.13.12+20260324-aarch64-pc-windows-msvc-install_only_stripped.tar.gz
```

items.title: Benchmax: hybrid search + 8x faster numpy insert + 1.8x faster than Zvec
items.body:
## Summary
Vectors as a column in Collection + hybrid text+vector search + numpy zero-copy insert.

## Insert Speed (d=128, N=10K)
| Method | Speed | vs JSON |
|---|---|---|
| JSON + extract embedding | 11.6K/s | 1x |
| ctypes array FFI | 59.5K/s | 5.1x |
| **numpy zero-copy** | **91.4K/s** | **7.9x** |

## Hybrid Search
- `col.configure_vectors(dims=1536, field="embedding")`
- `col.insert_with_embedding(key, text, np_embedding)` — numpy zero-copy
- `col.search_hybrid("query", embedding, text_weight=0.3, vector_weight=0.7)`
- Text pre-filter eliminates 99% → vector re-rank on survivors only

## vs Zvec (d=3072, N=100K)
- Search: TurboDB 22.5ms vs Zvec 40.5ms (**1.8x faster**)
- Insert: TurboDB 299K/s vs Zvec 17K/s (**18x faster**)

## Changes
- `src/collection.zig`: vectors field, configureVectors, insertWithEmbedding, searchVectors, searchHybrid, extractJsonFloatArray, fastParseFloat, parallel enableInt8
- `src/ffi.zig`: turbodb_insert_with_embedding, turbodb_configure_vectors, turbodb_search_hybrid
- `python/turbodb/__init__.py`: _float_ptr (numpy zero-copy), insert_with_embedding, search_hybrid
- `python/turbodb/_ffi.py`: ctypes prototypes

items.title: Detect screen lock/unlock and restart hotkeys on unlock
items.body:
## Root cause

Every time you walk away (screen locks), hotkeys break and the icon stays green. This is **not** the WH_KEYBOARD_LL hook dying — it's Windows corrupting the `keyboard` library's modifier state during lock. Ctrl/Shift can appear stuck-pressed, so combinations like `Ctrl+Space` never match even though the hook thread is still alive and delivering raw key events.

The existing sleep/wake detection only fires when the watchdog has a **gap > 30s** — screen lock without system sleep doesn't cause a gap, so it was invisible to the watchdog.

## Fix

Add `_is_screen_locked()` using `OpenInputDesktop`: when the session is locked, the input desktop is either inaccessible (returns 0) or named `"Winlogon"` instead of `"Default"`. Checked every 15s in the watchdog slow path. On the `locked → unlocked` transition, immediately restart the hotkey subprocess — fresh process = clean modifier state.

**No new dependencies.** Uses `ctypes.windll.user32` which is always available on Windows.

## What changes

- `import ctypes` added to `voice.py`
- `_is_screen_locked()` function added near the watchdog section
- `_screen_was_locked` bool tracked across watchdog ticks
- On unlock: `setup_hotkeys()` + `update_tray("#2ecc71", "Koda: Ready")`

## Test plan

- [x] 197 tests passing
- [ ] Lock screen (Win+L), wait a few seconds, unlock — hotkeys should work immediately
- [ ] Check `debug.log` for `"Screen unlock detected — restarting hotkeys to restore hook state"`
- [ ] Verify tray icon stays green (no false yellow) during normal use

items.title: Update wrappers with SWIG 4.4.1
Add C-accelerated pixel ops for millisecond-level thumbnail strip generation
items.body:
The no-cache contact-sheet path had unnecessary overhead: N intermediate BGRA buffer allocations, N separate ctypes calls for BGRA→RGB conversion, and Python-level bisect loops for keyframe snapping. For 4K videos this dominated post-extraction processing time.

### New C functions in `fast_thumb.c`

- **`split_strip_bgra_to_rgb`** — Combined split + BGRA→RGB in one pass over the strip. Replaces split → N×allocate → N×convert pipeline with a single C call.
- **`bgra_to_rgb_multi`** — Batch convert N concatenated BGRA frames in one ctypes call.
- **`rotate_bgra`** — 0/90/180/270° rotation with optional vflip. Available for replacing PIL `transpose()` in PyAV path.
- **`snap_to_keyframes`** — C binary search replacing Python bisect loop.
- **`scale_bilinear_bgra`** — Bilinear downscale for direct BGRA resizing.

### Integration

- **`worker.py`**: Contact-sheet caching now calls `_split_strip_bgra_to_rgb` (one C call for the entire strip→RGB conversion). Falls back through `bgra_to_rgb_multi` → per-frame → pure Python.
- **`extraction.py`**: `_snap_to_keyframes` delegates to C when available. New `_split_strip_bgra_to_rgb` wrapper exposed.
- **`_native/__init__.py`**: ctypes wrappers + pure-Python fallbacks for all new functions. JIT compilation adds `-lm`.

### Hot path before/after

```
Before: strip → C:split(N BGRA) → emit → N × C:bgra_to_rgb → join → cache
After:  strip → C:split(N BGRA) → emit → C:split_strip_bgra_to_rgb(1 call) → cache
```

18 new tests, 124 total passing. CodeQL clean.

items.title: fix: U-Boot 2024.01 build failures on modern hosts
items.body:
U-Boot 2024.01 fails to build on modern hosts due to two independent issues in `scripts/build-uboot.sh`.

## `ARCH=arm64` → `ARCH=arm`

U-Boot has no `arch/arm64/` — all ARM targets use `ARCH=arm`. `arm64` is a Linux kernel convention. Passing it caused `asm/config.h` (and everything depending on it) to be unresolvable at compile time.

## `libfdt_wrap.c` incompatible with SWIG ≥ 4.2.0

U-Boot ships a pre-generated `scripts/dtc/pylibfdt/libfdt_wrap.c` produced by an older SWIG. SWIG 4.2.0 added a third `int is_void` argument to `SWIG_Python_AppendOutput()`. On modern hosts the SWIG runtime header declares the 3-argument form, but the 6 call sites in `libfdt_wrap.c` use the old 2-argument form:

```c
// SWIG < 4.2.0 (what libfdt_wrap.c was generated with)
resultobj = SWIG_Python_AppendOutput(resultobj, val);

// SWIG ≥ 4.2.0 (what the host runtime header declares)
resultobj = SWIG_Python_AppendOutput(resultobj, val, 0);
```

A `sed` fixup is added inside the existing patch-stamp guard in `build-uboot.sh` to append `, 0` to each affected call site. The fix is idempotent and runs once per extracted source tree.

items.body:
## Changes Proposed

Move simple gossip handlers from C++ scripts to database conditions + SmartAI for 6 Northrend NPCs:

- **Iruk** (26219) — Quest-gated spell cast: shows option if quest 11961 incomplete, casts Create Totem of Issliruk on select
- **Deathstalker Razael** (23998) & **Dark Ranger Lyana** (23778) — Quest-gated creature credit for "Reports from the Field" (quest 11221). Gossip menu chains already wired in DB (8870→8869, 8879→8878). SAI gives TalkedToCreature credit on select
- **Roxi Ramrocket** (31247) — Spell-gated vendor: trainer option already in DB, adds vendor option conditioned on knowing Mechano-Hog (60866) or Mekgineer's Chopper (60867)
- **Avatar of Freya** (27801) — 3-step gossip chain already fully wired in DB (9720→9721→9722). Adds quest condition on first option (quest 12621) and SAI for final spell cast (52045)

### Minor behavioral note

The original C++ for Razael/Lyana checked `GetReqKillOrCastCurrentCount` to hide the gossip option after credit was already given. The DB condition only checks quest incomplete status, so the option remains visible until the quest is turned in. This is cosmetic only — selecting it again harmlessly re-gives credit.

## How Has This Been Tested?

Requires in-game testing:
- [ ] Iruk shows gossip option only with quest 11961 incomplete, creates totem on select
- [ ] Razael gives quest credit for objective 1 of quest 11221, shows response text
- [ ] Lyana gives quest credit for objective 2 of quest 11221, shows response text
- [ ] Roxi Ramrocket shows trainer option always, vendor option only if player knows Mechano-Hog or Mekgineer's Chopper
- [ ] Avatar of Freya shows 3-step gossip chain only with quest 12621 incomplete, casts spell on final step

## Issues Addressed

Continuation of #25207 and #25274 — moving hardcoded gossip handlers to database handling.

items.title: Sandbox: restrict network, filesystem, and import access in render_diagram
items.body:
## Summary

Harden the sandbox subprocess used by `render_diagram` with multiple layers of defense-in-depth:

- **Seccomp network blocking** (Linux): Blocks socket/connect/bind/listen/accept and related network syscalls via pyseccomp BPF filter. Fails closed on Linux if pyseccomp is missing.
- **Landlock filesystem restriction** (Linux 5.13+): Restricts filesystem access to tmpdir (rw) and system paths (ro) via direct Landlock syscalls (ctypes, zero new dependencies). Best-effort on older kernels.
- **Resource limits**: RLIMIT_CPU (30s), RLIMIT_FSIZE (50MB), RLIMIT_AS (512MB on Linux).
- **AST import validation**: Blocks imports of `os`, `subprocess`, `socket`, `http`, `ctypes`, `shutil`, and other dangerous modules. Wrapper globals cleaned before user code runs.
- **Process isolation**: `subprocess.Popen` with `start_new_session=True` and `os.killpg(SIGKILL)` on timeout to kill entire process trees. Python `-I` isolated mode. Minimal env (PATH, HOME, TMPDIR, LANG only).
- **CLI hardening**: `run_cli()` also uses Popen with session isolation and process-group killing.

## Test plan

- [x] All 225 existing tests pass (21 sandbox-specific)
- [x] New tests: `test_run_code_blocks_os_import`, `test_run_code_os_not_in_globals`
- [x] New Linux-only tests: `test_run_code_landlock_blocks_write_outside_tmpdir`, `test_run_code_landlock_allows_write_in_tmpdir`
- [x] Existing Linux-only tests: seccomp socket blocking, resource limits
- [x] `ruff check` passes cleanly
- [ ] Verify on Linux: Landlock blocks `/tmp` writes, seccomp blocks sockets, pyseccomp-missing raises RuntimeError

items.title: fix(folder_tool): resolve 5 test failures — Pillow compat + ctypes.windll patching
items.body:
## Summary

- **Pillow backward-compat**: `Image.Resampling.LANCZOS` was introduced in Pillow 9.1; the CI environment has 9.0.1 which only has `Image.LANCZOS`. Added `getattr(Image, "Resampling", Image).LANCZOS` shim in both `_load_ico_icon` and `_load_png_fallback` so source runs on any supported Pillow version.
- **ctypes.windll cross-platform patching**: `patch("ctypes.windll.shell32.SetCurrentProcessExplicitAppUserModelID")` raises `ModuleNotFoundError` on Linux because `windll` does not exist as a real attribute. Switched to `patch("ctypes.windll", mock_windll, create=True)` which injects the mock without touching the real ctypes namespace.
- **Import sort fix**: `model_generation/__init__.py` had an unsorted import block (I001) after new lazy-import lines were added in the staging pull; auto-fixed with `ruff check --fix`.

items.title: Fix: Resolve Linux startup crashes by decoupling window logic
items.body:
This pull request addresses critical crashes that prevented the FlexiTools application from launching natively on Linux.

**Changes Made:**
1. **DPI Awareness Guard:** Added an OS check to `main.py` so that the Windows-specific `ctypes.windll.shcore.SetProcessDpiAwareness(1)` call is completely bypassed on Linux.
2. **Decoupling Window Management:** Extracted the custom borderless window styling and native window dragging code from `ui.py` into a new, dedicated `window_utils.py` file.
3. **Graceful Fallbacks:** The new `window_utils.py` securely encapsulates the `ctypes` imports using `try...except ImportError`, ensuring that `ui.py` can import these functions safely across platforms. On Linux, it now falls back to standard Tkinter behaviors (`overrideredirect(True)` and manual dragging logic) rather than raising fatal `AttributeError` exceptions.

**Testing:**
- Verified via `xvfb-run python main.py` on Linux that the application successfully initializes and loads the main UI. 
- Windows functionality remains completely unchanged and has exact feature parity.

items.title: fix: use pure WTS ctypes API for session detection (fixes #183)
items.body:
## Problem
QA verification found that the previous fix (dcf691b) still fails in `schtasks /it` context because `query session` returns exit code 1, causing `_is_current_session_interactive()` to bail out prematurely.

## Root Cause
`query session` returns non-zero exit codes in many valid scenarios (schtasks, some RDP configs). Parsing its text output is also fragile across locales.

## Fix
Replace the entire `query session` subprocess approach with pure ctypes calls to `WTSQuerySessionInformationW` (WTS_INFO_CLASS = WTSConnectState). This:

1. Eliminates subprocess calls — no exit code or encoding issues
2. Directly queries the WTS connect state enum for the current session
3. Considers `WTSActive` (0) and `WTSConnected` (1) as interactive states
4. Works reliably in schtasks /it, RDP, console, and service contexts

## Testing
- All 1441 tests pass (475 skipped for platform-specific)
- Needs QA verification on compile machine in schtasks /it context

Fixes #183

items.title: Python rewrite: full IMRIS Coil QC pipeline (Phases 1–5 + file move)
items.body:
## Summary

Full Python implementation of the IMRIS Coil QC pipeline, replacing the MATLAB codebase. Covers DICOM reading, image analysis, SNR computation, drift detection, web backend, and post-processing file management.

## What's included

### Foundation (Phase 1)
- `config.py` — JSON config loader with strict type validation and MD5 provenance hash
- `storage/base.py` — `ResultStore` ABC (`save` / `load_history`)
- `storage/csv_store.py` — CSV backend with composite-key deduplication
- `dicom/parse_patient_name.py` — room + coil identity from PatientName field (e.g. `OR42Bot3Top5`)

### DICOM geometry & grouping (Phase 2)
- `dicom/coords.py` — slice normal, pixel-to-patient coords, angle between normals
- `dicom/group_planes.py` — greedy first-fit orientation clustering
- `dicom/compute_sos.py` — float64 sqrt(sum(I²)) coil combination
- `dicom/read_headers.py` — minimal header reader; coil ID from `(0051,100F)` Siemens private tag (confirmed against real Verio DICOM) with CSA `ImaCoilString` fallback

### Image analysis (Phase 3)
- `analysis/segment_phantom.py` — Otsu + largest central blob + hole fill + closing
- `analysis/estimate_noise.py` — Rayleigh MLE σ = `sqrt(mean(bg²)/2)`; NEMA `std(bg)` stored separately
- `analysis/select_noise_roi.py` — PE-direction ghost smear → 2 cm dilation → distance-transform ellipse placement
- `analysis/coil_signal_metrics.py` — max pixel inside phantom (IO1–IO8); mean inside 80%-eroded ellipse (COMBINED)
- `analysis/summarize_best_per_coil.py` — best-slice selection per coil element

### Drift detection (Phase 4)
- `analysis/compute_qc_deltas.py` — 30-day rolling median baseline, `delta_snr_pct`, `drift_flag` (threshold + absolute floor)

### Orchestration & watcher (Phase 5)
- `pipeline.py` — end-to-end orchestrator with `dry_run` support
- `watcher.py` — `watchdog`-based file watcher with 10 s settle-time heuristic; logs drift flags as warnings
- `main.py` — CLI entry point (`--config`, `--log-level`); NSSM-deployable Windows service

### Web backend & frontend (Phases 6–7)
- `web/auth.py` — Shibboleth SAML 2.0 SSO via `X-Remote-User` header; `IMRIS_DEV_USER` dev bypass
- `web/dicom_router.py` — `GET /api/dicom/{sop_uid}` → 16-bit PNG for Cornerstone.js
- `web/results_router.py` — results list/filter, single row, and `POST /manual-adjustment` (recompute SNR from user-supplied ellipses, insert `row_type=manual` row)
- `web/app.py` — FastAPI factory with CORS + static files
- `web/frontend/index.html` — single-page app: Cornerstone.js DICOM viewer (WW/WL drag), Konva.js editable ellipse ROI overlay, results table with drift flag highlighting, SNR trend chart

### Post-processing file move
- `io/move_series.py` — moves series directory to `PROCESSING_COMPLETED` or `PROCESSING_FAILED` after pipeline finishes; MD5-based collision handling; writes `manifest.json` audit record
- Config: new required fields `completed_dir` and `failed_dir`; `move_files: true` + `dry_run: false` enables moving

## Tests

227 tests, all passing. Key coverage:
- Config validation (type checking, bool/int distinction, missing fields)
- CSV store deduplication and history windowing
- PatientName regex parsing
- DICOM geometry (slice normal, pixel coords, angle, plane grouping)
- SoS computation
- DICOM header reading with mocked pydicom Dataset
- Phantom segmentation on synthetic images
- Rayleigh noise estimation (MLE accuracy, ratio to std)
- Noise ROI placement (PE exclusion, dilation, semi-axes)
- Signal metrics (max pixel, eroded mean)
- Best-slice selection with NaN SNR handling
- 30-day drift computation (median, delta %, flag logic)
- File move (simple, identical collision, different-content collision, manifest correctness)

## Config changes

```json
{
  "input_dir":     "C:\\IMRIS_Data\\FOR_PROCESSING",
  "completed_dir": "C:\\IMRIS_Data\\PROCESSING_COMPLETED",
  "failed_dir":    "C:\\IMRIS_Data\\PROCESSING_FAILED",
  "output_dir":    "C:\\IMRIS_Data\\output",
  ...
}
```

## Still needed (Phase 8)
- Integration testing with real DICOM files from the Verio
- Calibration of `snr_absolute_floor` default after first real data run
- Shibboleth SP nginx config for production deployment

items.title: fix: Pre-load c10.dll and import torch before PyQt
items.body:
- Implemented `ctypes.CDLL` manual pre-load of PyTorch's `c10.dll` explicitly for Windows platforms.
- Moved `import torch`, `ultralytics`, and `easyocr` to the absolute top of `main_ui.py`, ensuring they initialize *before* any `PyQt5` modules are imported.
- This addresses the notorious Windows DLL initializaton conflict (`WinError 1114`) where the PyQt GUI framework and heavy C++ PyTorch DLLs fight for the same primary initialization thread.

items.title: Add standalone Nanotec motor app, start scripts, and GUI/driver enhancements
items.body:
### Motivation
- Provide a small, standalone Tkinter application to operate only the Nanotec stepper motors and make it easy to start by double-clicking, while isolating the Nanotec backend from other device backends to avoid cross-device timeouts.
- Improve usability and diagnostics for motor operation by exposing absolute step counts, relative step ranges, jogging, step-zeroing, chamber labels and direction inversion for Motor 2, and clearer live CNC status.

### Description
- Added `python_rewrite/nanotec_motor_app.py` which implements `NanotecStandaloneRuntime`, constructs a `NanotecWindow`, loads runtime settings restricted to the Nanotec backend, runs a periodic tick loop and shows a message box on startup errors.
- Added platform start scripts `Start_Nanotec_Motors.bat` and `Start_Nanotec_Motors.command`, and updated `python_rewrite/README.md` with usage and notes for the new standalone app.
- Extended the motor model with `actual_position_steps` in `sputtering_app/models.py` and updated the Nanotec device driver (`sputtering_app/devices/nanotec.py`) to set and update `actual_position_steps` when reading positions and starting profiles.
- Large enhancements to `sputtering_app/nanotec_gui.py`: support custom `motor_order` and `chamber_labels`, an `invert M2 direction` toggle, CNC status panel, per-motor step-zero offsets, absolute/relative step display, jog buttons, step-range worker with dialog, step-dialog UI, improved taster mapping text, log trimming, UI label updates, and helper methods (`_effective_direction_for_motor`, `_move_motor_to_relative_step`, `_start_step_range`, etc.).

### Testing
- No automated tests were executed as part of this change.

items.title: Add Nanotec standalone Tkinter app and enhanced Nanotec GUI with step-count features
items.body:
### Motivation

- Provide a lightweight, standalone Tkinter host for controlling only the Nanotec stepper motors without opening the full application.
- Improve Nanotec motor model and GUI to expose absolute/relative step counts, manual step-range/jog controls and clearer chamber labeling to match operator needs.
- Ensure the GUI remains safe by routing all hardware actions through the central `Controller` and preserving existing safety checks.

### Description

- Added a standalone runtime `python_rewrite/nanotec_motor_app.py` that launches a `NanotecWindow` bound to the main `Controller` and runtime settings loader.
- Added platform start scripts `Start_Nanotec_Motors.bat` and `Start_Nanotec_Motors.command` and documented them in `python_rewrite/README.md`.
- Extended the motor data model with `actual_position_steps: int` in `models.MotorState` and updated the Nanotec device implementation to populate `actual_position_steps` when querying and starting profiles in `devices/nanotec.py`.
- Substantially enhanced `nanotec_gui.NanotecWindow` to support configurable motor ordering and chamber labels, display absolute and relative step counts, set a step zero, manual jog, step-range automation (with a background thread), step dialog, progress display and taster-mapping text; added direction translation helpers to account for inverted motor wiring.
- Minor UI and robustness improvements: threading import, window title adjustment, safer controller swaps when applying new runtime settings, and logging of events to reuse the existing message pane.

### Testing

- Ran the project's automated unit test suite with `pytest` (existing tests only); the run completed with all tests green.

## Summary
- Complete rewrite of `examples/approval-workflow/python/main.py` from Model A (manual orchestration) to **Model B (ScenarioBundle + server-driven workflow)**
- All 4 scenarios defined as `ScenarioBundle` dicts with agents, humans, workflow steps (`step_deadline_seconds`, `human_task`, `remind_after_seconds`, `max_reminders`)
- Client reduced to: **one `apply_scenario()` call + `observe()` event loop**
- Human step handled via `intent.human_task_assigned` event — client pauses, reads Enter, calls `resume_intent(task_result={"approved": true})`
- Full server audit trail printed at end via `list_intent_events()`
- `TIMED_OUT` treated as terminal status alongside `COMPLETED`/`FAILED`

## What the server now owns (no longer in client code)
- Agent provisioning (create_if_missing in bundle)
- Workflow DAG compilation and step transitions
- `pending_with` updates from DAG `assigned_to`
- Deadline enforcement (`deadline_at`)
- Reminder scheduling (`remind_after_seconds`, `max_reminders`)
- Per-step timeout (`step_deadline_seconds`)

## Test plan

- [ ] `SCENARIO=1 python main.py` runs without picker
- [ ] `[agent:create]` / `[agent:assign]` lines appear for all 3 SA agents

items.title: Python bus rewrite: agent lifecycle + request/reply + sessions
items.body:
## Summary

Full Python rewrite of khonliang-bus. Implements three bundled Step 1 FRs from the agent platform migration roadmap (see `khonliang-developer/specs/bus-mcp/architecture.md`):

- **Python rewrite** — FastAPI + WebSocket + SQLite replaces the Go v0.1 server. Same language as the rest of the ecosystem so the flow engine (Step 6) can use khonliang primitives (AgentTeam, ConsensusEngine) directly.
- **Request/reply + queue semantics** — `POST /v1/request` routes to agent callback URLs with configurable retry (exponential backoff), dead letter on max failures, NACK support. Pub/sub retained.
- **Agent lifecycle** — install/uninstall (persistent), register/deregister/heartbeat (runtime), start/stop/restart, PID crash detection, health tracking.

Also includes sessions (create/suspend/resume/archive with public/private context), execution traces with correlation IDs, and platform status.

## Endpoints

| Group | Endpoints |
|---|---|
| Lifecycle | POST install, DELETE install/{id}, GET install, start/stop/restart |
| Registration | POST register, deregister, heartbeat, GET services |
| Request/reply | POST request (with retry + dead letter) |
| Pub/sub | POST publish, ack, nack, WS subscribe |
| Sessions | POST session, GET session/{id}, message, suspend, resume, DELETE |
| Observability | GET health, status, trace/{id} |

## Test plan

- [x] 49/49 tests passing across 6 test files
- [x] Zero deprecation warnings
- [x] Manual smoke test: boot server, install agent, register with skills, request/reply (fails gracefully when no real agent), publish, create session, suspend/resume, deregister
- [ ] Copilot review
